data.title: Has NiSource (NI) Run Too Far After Its 26% One Year Share Price Gain
data.description:

  • NiSource shares last closed at US$48.08, with returns of 0.2% over 7 days, 1.2% over 30 days, 14.0% year to date and 25.6% over the past year. These figures can influence how investors think about both upside potential and downside risk.
  • Recent coverage has focused on NiSource's role as a regulated utility and how its capital plans, regulatory relationships and balance sheet position frame expectations for future cash flows. That backdrop helps explain why some investors are reassessing what counts as a reasonable price for the stock right now.
  • Even so, NiSource currently scores 0 out of 6 on our valuation checks. The next sections will compare different valuation methods to unpack that score and then finish with a more holistic way to think about what the stock might be worth.

Approach 1: NiSource Dividend Discount Model (DDM) Analysis

The Dividend Discount Model estimates what NiSource might be worth today by projecting future dividends and discounting them back to a present value. It is essentially asking whether the stream of expected dividend payments justifies the current share price.

For NiSource, the model uses an annual dividend per share of about US$1.34, a return on equity of 9.07% and a payout ratio of roughly 61.60%. The implied long term dividend growth rate is capped at 3.41%, which is close to the model’s calculated expected growth of 3.48%. That growth cap is intended to keep assumptions for a regulated utility stock on the conservative side and avoid overly aggressive projections.

Putting those inputs together, the DDM output points to an estimated intrinsic value of about US$37.62 per share. Compared with the recent share price of US$48.08, the DDM indicates NiSource trades at roughly a 27.8% premium to this dividend based estimate, which screens as overvalued on this model alone.

Result: OVERVALUED

What counts as a “normal” P/E depends on how the market weighs growth potential and risk. Higher expected earnings growth or lower perceived risk can justify a higher multiple, while slower growth or higher risk usually lines up with a lower one.

data.title: Is It Too Late To Consider General Motors (GM) After A 69% One Year Rally?
data.description:
• If you are wondering whether General Motors at around US$75.77 is still offering value after a strong run, the answer depends on how you look at what the market is pricing in today.
  • The stock has had a mixed short term patch, with a 2.9% decline over the last week, a 4.5% gain over the last 30 days, a 6.4% year to date decline, and a 69.0% return over the last year, alongside a 135.1% return over three years and 33.4% over five years.
  • Recent coverage has focused on General Motors as a key Auto name, with investors weighing its position in the industry, capital allocation choices, and long term product roadmap. Headlines have also repeatedly returned to questions about how effectively the company is using its balance sheet and investment budget to support future competitiveness.
  • On Simply Wall St’s valuation checklist, General Motors scores 5 out of 6 for being undervalued, and the rest of this article will walk through what different valuation methods are implying, before finishing with a framework that can help you pull these numbers into a clearer story about the stock.

General Motors delivered 69.0% returns over the last year. See how this stacks up to the rest of the Auto industry.

Approach 1: General Motors Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow model takes the cash General Motors is expected to generate in the future and then discounts those projected cash flows back into today’s dollars to estimate what the entire business might be worth right now.

For General Motors, the latest twelve-month free cash flow sits at about $13.6b. The current DCF model uses a 2-stage Free Cash Flow to Equity approach, with analyst projections feeding into the earlier years and Simply Wall St extrapolating further out. For example, projected free cash flow is $10.2b in 2026, $12.1b in 2027, and $12.2b in 2028, with estimates then extended out to 2035, where projected free cash flow is $14.0b.

After discounting all of those projected cash flows back to today, the model arrives at an estimated intrinsic value of about $132.21 per share. Against a recent share price of roughly $75.77, that implies the stock is about 42.7% undervalued on this DCF view.

Result: UNDERVALUED

Approach 2: General Motors Price vs Earnings

For profitable companies, the P/E ratio is a useful shorthand for how much investors are currently paying for each dollar of earnings. It ties directly to what you care about as a shareholder, the earnings that can eventually be paid out or reinvested.

What counts as a “normal” or “fair” P/E depends on how fast earnings are expected to grow and how risky those earnings are. Higher expected growth and lower perceived risk can justify a higher P/E, while slower growth or higher risk usually point to a lower multiple.

General Motors currently trades on a P/E of 28.1x. That sits above the Auto industry average of 18.7x and below the peer average of 55.6x. Simply Wall St’s “Fair Ratio” for General Motors is 31.7x, which is a proprietary estimate of the P/E that might be reasonable given the company’s earnings profile, industry, profit margins, market cap and risk factors.

This Fair Ratio aims to be more tailored than a simple comparison against peers or the industry, because it adjusts for company specific traits rather than assuming all Auto names deserve the same multiple. With the actual P/E of 28.1x below the Fair Ratio of 31.7x, General Motors screens as undervalued on this earnings multiple view.

data.title: Seiko Epson (TSE:6724) Margin Compression Reinforces Bearish Profitability Narratives
data.description:
Seiko Epson (TSE:6724) has put out a solidly detailed FY 2026 update, with third quarter revenue at ¥376.5 billion and basic EPS at ¥52.37, while trailing 12 month figures show revenue at about ¥1.38 trillion and EPS at ¥134.84. Over recent periods the company has seen revenue move from ¥339.1 billion in Q4 FY 2025 to ¥376.5 billion in Q3 FY 2026, with quarterly EPS shifting from ¥24.56 to ¥52.37 as net income moved from ¥7,798 million to ¥16,781 million over the same stretch. For investors, the key question is how these earnings and the pressure on margins fit with the medium growth outlook and dividend profile.

See our full analysis for Seiko Epson.

With the headline numbers on the table, the next step is to set these results against the most common narratives around Seiko Epson to see which views are supported by the data and which might need a rethink.

Curious how numbers become stories that shape markets? Explore Community Narratives

Advertisement

TTM net margin slips to 3.1%

  • Over the last 12 months, net profit margin moved from 4.3% to 3.1%, while trailing net income sat at ¥43,243 million on about ¥1.38b of revenue.
  • Critics highlight a bearish concern that legacy hardware is struggling, and the margin data partly backs that view:
    • Five year earnings are described as having declined about 8.5% per year, which fits the idea of profit pressure in more mature product lines.
    • At the same time, revenue is forecast to grow around 2.5% per year, so the squeeze is coming more from profitability than from the top line alone.

Mid single digit earnings outlook vs Japan

  • Analysts expect earnings growth of roughly 6.75% per year, compared with a 10.1% earnings growth forecast for the wider Japanese market and revenue growth expectations of around 2.5% per year for Seiko Epson.
  • Supporters of a more bullish angle see the multi segment business mix as a positive, and the numbers give a mixed read on that idea:
    • On one hand, the company is described as having high quality past earnings, which lines up with the view that a diversified tech industrial can produce relatively steady profits over time.
    • On the other hand, the 8.5% annual decline in earnings over five years shows that diversification alone has not kept profit trends in line with the broader Japanese growth profile.

Valuation gap and 3.53% dividend yield

  • Against a share price of ¥2,095, the stock sits below a DCF fair value of ¥3,264.26 and offers a 3.53% dividend yield, while the 15.5x P/E is below the 23.4x Asian Tech industry average but above the 11.6x peer group figure.
  • What is interesting for a bullish narrative check is how these valuation marks line up with the recent profit softness:
    • The roughly 35.8% gap to DCF fair value and the dividend yield suggest the current price already reflects some caution about the 3.1% net margin and the five year earnings decline.
    • However, the fact that the P/E is lower than the wider Asian Tech group but higher than direct peers shows the market is pricing in some quality premium, even with only mid single digit earnings growth expected.

data.title: Cboe Global Markets (CBOE) Margin Strength And 43.9% EPS Growth Test Slower Growth Narrative
data.description:
Cboe Global Markets (CBOE) just opened Q1 2026 earnings season with a solid set of headline figures, anchored by Q4 2025 revenue of US$1.2b and basic EPS of US$2.98, alongside trailing twelve month revenue of US$4.7b and EPS of US$10.46 that reflect its latest run rate. Over recent quarters the company has seen revenue move from US$1.11b in Q4 2024 to US$1.20b in Q4 2025, while quarterly EPS shifted from US$1.87 to US$2.98. This sets up a story where higher earnings and a 23.2% trailing net margin give investors a results season focused on profitability quality and staying power.

See our full analysis for Cboe Global Markets.

With the raw numbers on the table, the next step is to see how this earnings profile lines up against the key narratives around Cboe’s growth, risks, and overall profitability story.

See what the community is saying about Cboe Global Markets

Advertisement

Margins Hold At 23.2% While Growth Slows

  • Cboe’s trailing net profit margin sits at 23.2%, compared with 18.6% a year earlier, alongside trailing twelve month revenue of US$4.7b and net income of about US$1.1b.
  • What stands out for the bullish narrative is that recent 43.9% earnings growth and a five year earnings growth rate of 23.3% per year sit next to forecast revenue and earnings growth of only about 4.2% per year. This heavily supports the idea that past expansion in derivatives and data is already visible in the numbers, but also tests how much further margin gains can realistically go if top line growth slows to those forecast levels.
    • Bulls point to high margin, recurring data and index licensing revenue as a driver of earnings quality, and the current 23.2% net margin against last year’s 18.6% fits that story of stronger profitability.
    • At the same time, forecasts of around 4.2% annual growth in both revenue and earnings are much lower than the 43.9% earnings growth just reported over the past year, which challenges the idea that the recent pace can be extrapolated.
Over the last year Cboe’s expanding margins and earnings growth have made the bullish case look very compelling. If that is the view you lean toward it is worth seeing how supporters connect these numbers to future growth in more detail in the 🐂 Cboe Global Markets Bull Case.

P/E At 31.3x With DCF Fair Value Below Price

  • The shares trade on a 31.3x P/E at a price of US$326.96, compared with a US Capital Markets industry average of 42.4x and a peer average of 29.7x, while the DCF fair value cited is US$282.39.
  • Bears highlight that the share price is above the DCF fair value and only modestly below the 31.3x P/E implied by the 303.86 analyst price target. This fits a cautious view that a lot of the recent 43.9% earnings growth and 23.2% margin is already reflected in the valuation and that slower forecast growth of about 4.2% per year could limit upside from here.
    • The gap between the current US$326.96 share price and the US$282.39 DCF fair value provides a concrete reference point for those arguing the shares screen as expensive on that metric.
    • With the analyst price target of 303.86 sitting below today’s price and forecasts calling for mid single digit growth, skeptics can reasonably question how much room is left if growth normalises to those levels.
Skeptical investors often focus on the spread between price, fair value estimates, and slower forecast growth. If that sounds like your concern it is worth seeing how that case is laid out in full in the 🐻 Cboe Global Markets Bear Case.

LTM EPS Of US$10.46 Underpins Mixed Growth Story

  • On a trailing basis, EPS totals US$10.46 with trailing twelve month revenue of US$4.7b, compared with trailing revenue of US$4.1b and net income of US$761m a year ago.
  • Consensus narrative points to broad based growth in derivatives, data, and global trading, and the trailing numbers back that up on profitability. Yet the combination of 23.2% net margin, 43.9% earnings growth, and forecasts of roughly 4.2% annual revenue and earnings growth gives you a mixed picture where the recent past looks strong but the forward pace embedded in models is much more measured.
    • The move from US$4.1b to US$4.7b in trailing revenue and from US$761m to about US$1.1b in trailing net income lines up with the view that data and international segments have been important earnings contributors.
    • However, analysts expecting earnings to reach about US$1.4b by around 2029 with profit margins rising to 46.3% are assuming a very different phase compared with the last 12 months, so it helps to think about whether that shift from high growth to slower, higher margin expansion matches your own expectations.

Next Steps

Mixed signals on growth and valuation can be confusing, so it helps to review the numbers, weigh both sides, and decide what fits your plan. To see how the current story balances optimism with concern, take a close look at the 3 key rewards and 1 important warning sign.

See What Else Is Out There
